cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life, especially following injury or learning

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The area in the left temporal lobe responsible for language comprehension; damage causes difficulty understanding speech

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The strip of cortex at the back of the frontal lobe that controls voluntary muscle movement

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The thick band of nerve fibers connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres, allowing communication between them

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The 'master gland' of the endocrine system located in the brain that regulates other glands and releases hormones controlling growth, stress, and reproduction

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The outermost layer of the brain responsible for higher-order thinking, reasoning, perception, and voluntary movement

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A network of neurons in the brainstem that filters incoming stimuli and regulates arousal, wakefulness, and attention

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The cerebral hemisphere dominant for language, logic, and analytical processing in most right-handed people

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A stimulant drug that blocks the reuptake of dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin, producing intense euphoria and heightened alertness

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A language disorder caused by brain damage that impairs the ability to produce or understand speech

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The strip of cortex in the parietal lobe that receives and processes body sensory information such as touch, temperature, and pain

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The cerebral hemisphere dominant for spatial abilities, facial recognition, artistic expression, and emotional processing

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A psychoactive drug derived from the cannabis plant that acts on cannabinoid receptors, producing altered perception, relaxation, and impaired short-term memory

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The physical and/or psychological symptoms that occur when a person who is dependent on a drug stops or reduces use

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A psychoactive drug classified as a CNS depressant that at low doses reduces inhibitions and at high doses impairs coordination, judgment, and memory

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A mild stimulant drug that blocks adenosine receptors, increasing alertness and reducing fatigue

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The electrical state of a neuron at rest (approximately -70 mV) when it is not firing an action potential

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A research method that compares identical (monozygotic) and fraternal (dizygotic) twins to assess the relative contributions of genes and environment to a trait

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The process by which heritable traits that increase reproductive success become more common in a population over successive generations

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense that provides information about body position and movement of limbs through receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of balance and spatial orientation provided by the semicircular canals and vestibular sacs in the inner ear

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The experience of pain or sensation in a limb that has been amputated, believed to result from ongoing neural activity in the brain's body map

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that pain signals can be blocked ('gated') by non-pain input and that the spinal cord acts as a neurological gate controlling pain transmission to the brain

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Small bumps on the tongue containing taste receptor cells (taste buds); responsible for detecting the five basic tastes

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain structure located above the nasal cavity that receives and processes smell signals from olfactory receptor neurons

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of taste; involves detecting chemical stimuli via taste receptor cells on the tongue and soft palate

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of smell; involves detecting airborne chemical molecules via olfactory receptor neurons in the nasal cavity

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Hearing loss caused by damage to the inner ear (cochlea) or auditory nerve, affecting the ability to convert sound waves into neural signals

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Hearing loss caused by damage to or obstruction of the outer or middle ear structures that conduct sound to the inner ear

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The ability to determine the direction of a sound source using differences in the timing and intensity of sound waves reaching each ear

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that pitch perception is determined by the rate of neural firing in the auditory nerve, with higher pitches producing faster firing (best for low-frequency sounds)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The electrochemical signal that travels down a neuron's axon when the neuron is sufficiently stimulated; follows the all-or-none principle

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The principle that a neuron either fires with full strength or does not fire at all — there is no partial firing

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The branching extensions of a neuron that receive signals from other neurons and transmit them toward the cell body

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The fatty tissue layer that encases and insulates axons, speeding up the transmission of electrical signals

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The junction between the axon terminal of one neuron and the dendrite or cell body of the next neuron, across which neurotransmitters are released

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neurotransmitter associated with reward, motivation, and motor control; depleted in Parkinson's disease; excess linked to schizophrenia

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neurotransmitter that regulates mood, appetite, and sleep; low levels associated with depression

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Natural opioid-like neurotransmitters released by the brain that reduce pain and produce feelings of pleasure

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The principal inhibitory neurotransmitter in the CNS that reduces neural excitability; benzodiazepines enhance its effects

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The part of the limbic system that plays a key role in processing emotional responses, especially fear and threat detection

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The limbic system structure critical for forming new long-term memories; damage causes anterograde amnesia

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain structure that acts as a relay station for sensory information (except smell), routing signals to the appropriate cortical areas

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The minimum stimulation needed to detect a particular stimulus 50% of the time

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The minimum difference between two stimuli required for detection 50% of the time; also called the just noticeable difference (JND)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Conducted split-brain research with epileptic patients, demonstrating that the two cerebral hemispheres have specialized functions and cannot communicate when the corpus callosum is severed

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Discovered the brain area responsible for speech production in the left frontal lobe by studying patients with aphasia; the area now bears his name

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Identified the area in the left temporal lobe responsible for language comprehension; patients with damage there produce fluent but meaningless speech

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Proposed the gate control theory of pain, arguing that the spinal cord acts as a gate that can be opened or closed to pain signals based on competing input

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A patient with damage to the left temporal lobe can speak fluently but produces sentences that make no sense. Which condition does this most likely describe?

choices.explanation: Wernicke's aphasia results from damage to Wernicke's area in the left temporal lobe, causing fluent but meaningless speech because language comprehension is impaired.
Broca's aphasia causes effortful, halting speech — patients struggle to speak but comprehension is relatively intact, the opposite of this scenario.
Global aphasia involves both production and comprehension deficits — the patient described here can still produce fluent speech.
Conduction aphasia involves difficulty repeating words despite relatively intact comprehension and spontaneous speech, not the fluent meaningless speech described here.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: After suffering a stroke, a patient cannot move the right side of their body. Based on contralateral control, which part of the brain most likely sustained damage?

choices.explanation: The motor cortex controls the opposite (contralateral) side of the body — the left motor cortex controls right-side movement, so damage there would paralyze the right side.
The right motor cortex controls the left side of the body; damage there would cause left-side paralysis, not right-side paralysis.
The somatosensory cortex processes sensory input rather than controlling movement; damage there would impair sensation, not motor control.
The cerebellum coordinates balance and fine motor movements rather than initiating voluntary movement; damage typically causes ataxia, not hemiplegia.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Which type of brain imaging technique measures changes in blood flow to identify areas of heightened neural activity during a cognitive task?

choices.explanation: Functional MRI (fMRI) detects changes in blood oxygenation (BOLD signal) to map which brain regions are most active during a task, providing excellent spatial resolution.
EEG measures electrical activity (brain waves) and has excellent temporal resolution but poor spatial resolution — it does not directly measure blood flow.
PET scans measure metabolic activity using radioactive tracers, but fMRI is the more common real-time method and does not require injection of radioactive substances.
Structural MRI produces detailed images of brain anatomy but does not measure functional activity or blood flow changes during tasks.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A person who frequently uses heroin now requires a larger dose to achieve the same effect. Which phenomenon does this demonstrate?

choices.explanation: Tolerance occurs when repeated drug use causes the brain to adapt, requiring increasingly larger doses to achieve the same effect as receptor sensitivity decreases.
Withdrawal describes symptoms that occur when drug use stops — not the reduced response to the same dose while still using the drug.
Sensitization is the opposite of tolerance — an increased response to the same dose; this scenario describes decreased response requiring higher doses.
Psychological dependence involves craving and compulsion to use — the scenario specifically describes a physiological adaptation requiring higher doses, which is tolerance.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Which nervous system subdivision is primarily responsible for slowing the heart rate, stimulating digestion, and conserving energy?

choices.explanation: The parasympathetic division ('rest and digest') slows the heart rate, stimulates digestion, and conserves energy — the opposite of the fight-or-flight response.
The sympathetic division ('fight or flight') accelerates heart rate and suppresses digestion to prepare the body for action — opposite of what is described.
The somatic nervous system controls voluntary muscle movements, not the involuntary functions like heart rate and digestion described here.
The CNS processes information and directs responses, but the specific automatic body-maintenance functions described here are carried out by the autonomic PNS.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A musician who practiced violin for 20 years has a larger cortical representation of the left hand's fingers in the somatosensory cortex than a non-musician. Which concept best explains this?

choices.explanation: Brain plasticity (neuroplasticity) describes the brain's ability to reorganize and grow new neural connections in response to experience — repeated use of the left hand expands its cortical representation.
Lateralization refers to specialization of the left and right hemispheres for different functions — it does not explain increased cortical area from practice.
The all-or-none principle describes how neurons either fire fully or not at all — it is a property of individual neuron firing, not long-term cortical reorganization.
Long-term potentiation is a cellular mechanism for memory formation (synaptic strengthening), not the macro-level cortical reorganization (expanded brain map area) described here.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A researcher finds that identical twins raised apart show very similar IQ scores. Which conclusion is best supported by this finding?

choices.text: Genetic factors contribute significantly to intelligence
choices.explanation: Identical (monozygotic) twins share 100% of their genes — similar IQ scores despite different environments strongly implicates genetic influence on intelligence.

choices.text: Environment determines intelligence more than genes
choices.explanation: If environment were the primary determinant, twins raised in different environments would show more divergent IQ scores; similarity despite different environments points to genes.

choices.text: Intelligence is equally determined by genes and environment
choices.explanation: While both contribute, this specific finding — similarity despite environmental differences — primarily implicates genetics, not equal contribution.

choices.text: The heritability of intelligence is zero
choices.explanation: Heritability of zero would mean genes don't contribute at all, predicting no more similarity between identical twins than random pairs — contradicting the finding.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A drug that mimics a neurotransmitter by binding to and activating receptor sites is best classified as which of the following?

choices.explanation: An agonist enhances or mimics a neurotransmitter's effect by binding to and activating receptor sites, producing the same or greater response than the neurotransmitter itself.
An antagonist blocks receptor sites, preventing the neurotransmitter from binding and reducing or eliminating its effect — the opposite of activation.
A reuptake inhibitor blocks reabsorption of a neurotransmitter into the presynaptic neuron, increasing its presence in the synapse — different from directly activating receptor sites.
A second messenger is an intracellular signaling molecule activated after a neurotransmitter binds — it is not the classification for drugs that activate receptor sites.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A researcher uses a technique with high temporal resolution but limited spatial resolution to measure brain activity. Which technique is being used?

choices.explanation: EEG records brain electrical activity in real time with millisecond precision but cannot precisely localize which deep brain structure is generating the signal.
fMRI has excellent spatial resolution (can identify specific brain regions) but relatively poor temporal resolution — it measures blood flow changes that lag neural activity by seconds.
PET scans measure metabolic activity over minutes, providing even poorer temporal resolution than fMRI, though with reasonable spatial resolution.
CT scans produce structural images of the brain using X-rays — they do not measure ongoing neural activity at all.
